topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed specifically calibrated to grate on my nerves. I stared at the application form, the ink blurring under my gaze. Liam had applied for the Senior Analyst position, and I had just submitted mine. It felt like a deliberate, cruel joke orchestrated by the universe. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet cubicle farm. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the ache in my molars. Why couldn't he just be… less competitive? I stood up abruptly, pacing the cramped space, my hands flexing at my sides. Every perfectly organized file on my desk seemed to mock my current state of agitation. I needed air. I needed to physically move away from this suffocating proximity to his success.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the word tasting like ash in my mouth. I reread the job description, the same exacting requirements that Liam had clearly breezed through. A hot flush crept up my neck, making my skin feel prickly. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to contain the tremor starting in my hands. It was infuriating. It wasn't just about the job; it was about the implication—that I was somehow less deserving, less capable, than him. I picked up my pen, intending to furiously rewrite a section of my cover letter, but the ink just seemed to swim. I shoved the paper aside, the sudden movement causing a stack of papers to shift precariously. I let out a slow, ragged breath, my chest rising and falling unevenly, a tight knot forming just beneath my sternum.

[story 3]

I watched Liam walk past my desk, his easy confidence radiating across the room like heat. He offered a casual nod, a small, self-satisfied smile playing on his lips. The sight of him, so effortlessly succeeding where I felt I was perpetually stumbling, tightened something deep inside me. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white, the wood digging painfully into my palms. My breathing became shallow and quick. *He gets it. He always gets it.* The thought spiraled into a churning vortex of resentment. I picked up my phone, intending to call a colleague, but my fingers hovered over the screen, paralyzed by a sudden, overwhelming urge to simply destroy the document in front of me. The sheer unfairness of the situation was pressing down on my chest, making every inhale feel like a conscious, painful effort.
